rule_text: A trader must measure and manage transaction costs, understand price efficiency, and quantify volatility exposure before executing trades, adjusting position size and trade frequency based on these three factors.
entry_conditions: Transaction costs (bid-ask spread + commissions + slippage) < 0.5% of position value
Price efficiency level (measured by price responsiveness to news) is HIGH
Volatility (measured as rolling 20-day ATR % or implied vol percentile) is BELOW 50th percentile
exit_conditions: Any single condition deteriorates below threshold
Transaction costs exceed 0.75% realized on position

Trading is a zero-sum game in an important accounting sense. In a zero-sum game, the total
gains of the winners are exactly equal to the total losses of the losers. Trading is a zero-sum
game because
rule_text: A trader should identify and trade against counterparties whose motivations for trading differ from profit maximization, because understanding loser behavior reveals profitable trading opportunities.
entry_conditions: Identify at least 2 distinct non-profit trading motivations in order flow (e.g., forced liquidations + index rebalancing)
Confirm counterparty trading direction contradicts price momentum for 3+ consecutive periods
Position size of identified losers exceeds 60% of total order flow in that direction
exit_conditions: Counterparty motivation changes or disappears (e.g., rebalancing ends)
Price reaches 2 standard deviations from entry
Time stop: 10 trading days

Transaction cost to one side is trading profit to the other side. Traders who demand liquidity
tend to pay transaction costs while those who offer liquidity have negative transaction costs.
The Flip S
rule_text: A trader should minimize costs when demanding liquidity and maximize revenue opportunity when offering liquidity, recognizing that transaction costs paid by one side are profits to the other.

exit_conditions: Spread widens beyond 2x historical average

Edge conditions change materially

The Price Accelerator
Increases in prices transfer wealth from pessimistic traders who have short positions to optimistic
traders who have long positions. These transfers can cause accelerate price ch
rule_text: Price acceleration occurs when wealth transfers from losing traders force them to take corrective actions (covering margin calls or reducing positions), creating self-reinforcing price momentum. A trader should enter in the direction of price movement when conditions suggest forced buying/selling will amplify the trend.
entry_conditions: Price in uptrend (close > 20-period SMA)
Acceleration rate > 1.5 standard deviations of recent price changes
Volume > 150% of 20-period average (indicates forced liquidation activity)
Rate of change of momentum > 0 (acceleration itself accelerating)
exit_conditions: Acceleration rate falls below 0.8 standard deviations
Volume reverts to < 110% of average
Stop loss at 2% below entry

raw_chunk_text:
You Know You Are Right, But …
(Timing Is Everything)
Consider the trade timing decisions that value traders must make when they believe that prices
are too high:
If they initially have long positions,
rule_text: A trader with conviction in a directional thesis must balance conviction against forced liquidation risk by calibrating entry timing to preserve sufficient capital buffer, because premature entry risks margin call before the thesis can materialize.
entry_conditions: Conviction thesis is established (fundamental or technical)
Available margin buffer >= 40% of notional position size
Price has moved 5%+ in thesis direction (confirmation signal)
exit_conditions: Thesis invalidated by price action
Margin utilization exceeds 70%
